critique.summary: The article introduces Qite.js, a frontend JavaScript framework designed for developers who dislike React and prefer working directly with HTML. It emphasizes a no-build, no-NPM, server-side rendering (SSR)-first approach that manipulates the real DOM directly without using a virtual DOM or complex build tools.
mainPoints: Qite.js avoids the complexity of modern frontend frameworks by treating the DOM as the source of truth and manipulating it directly.
It requires no build step, no virtual DOM, no JSX, no transpiler, and no bundler, enabling direct use in the browser with plain HTML and JavaScript.
Qite.js supports SSR-first applications with the ability to enhance pages with SPA-like behavior selectively.
The framework uses declarative state rules, a hierarchy-driven event system, and separates markup, behavior, and styling clearly.
Components communicate through explicit events, and dynamic components use native HTML <template> elements.
Qite.js is positioned as a lightweight, self-sufficient alternative to React and other modern frameworks, without requiring npm or a large dependency ecosystem.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Qite.js align with the information available on its official website and documentation. It is accurate that Qite.js is a lightweight framework focusing on direct DOM manipulation without a virtual DOM or build step, and it supports SSR-first development. The example code and feature descriptions are consistent with typical frontend framework designs that prioritize simplicity and minimal tooling. However, since Qite.js is a niche or emerging framework, it is less widely known or adopted compared to React or Vue, and independent third-party reviews or comparisons are limited. The article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, so while the technical claims appear accurate, the promotional tone and lack of external validation should be noted.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential limitations or trade-offs of avoiding a virtual DOM, such as performance implications in complex applications.
There is no comparison with other frameworks beyond React, so readers lack context on how Qite.js performs or scales relative to alternatives.
The framework's ecosystem, community support, and long-term maintenance are not addressed, which are important for production use decisions.
Security considerations and compatibility with modern web standards or browsers are not mentioned.
The article does not provide user testimonials, benchmarks, or case studies to substantiate claims of being 'powerful enough for serious apps.'

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the complexity introduced by modern reactive UI frameworks, with some commenters appreciating Qite.js's no-build, SSR-first approach as a return to simpler web development principles. Others express skepticism about abandoning JavaScript or React-like components entirely, noting trade-offs in complexity and practicality for large applications.

comments.author: hliyan
comments.text: I'm starting to wonder whether reactivity (not React specifically) was the originally sin that led to modern UI complexity. UI elements automatically reacting to data changes (as oppposed to components updating themselves by listening to events) was supposed to make things easier. But in reality, it introduced state as something distinct from both the UI and the data source (usually an API or a local cache). That introduced state management. It was all downhill from there (starting with two way data binding, Flux architecture, Redux, state vs. props, sagas, prop drilling, hooks, context API, stateful components vs. stateless components, immutability, shallow copy vs. deep copy, so on and so forth).
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully critiques the evolution of UI frameworks and the complexity introduced by reactive state management, a widely recognized challenge in frontend development.

comments.author: rsmtjohn
comments.text:
I have been building with Next.js for years and still reach for it by default, but I genuinely appreciate the philosophy here. The no-build approach eliminates an entire class of problems -- stale caches, bundler config drift, slow CI pipelines -- that we have collectively normalized as "just how frontend works."

The SSR-first angle is especially interesting. Most frameworks bolt on SSR as an afterthought. Starting from HTML and progressively enhancing is how the web was designed to work, and it is telling that we keep circling back to it.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately highlights practical benefits of no-build SSR-first frameworks and aligns with current trends emphasizing progressive enhancement.

comments.author: rounce
comments.text: Why have `<div data-part="form">` instead of using a `<form>` element?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid semantic HTML concern that could impact accessibility and standard compliance, encouraging better markup practices.

comments.author: dmix
comments.text: Rails does this perfectly with Turbo and Stimulus, Turbo does HTML-over-the-wire via SSR-first, server responds with small updates/appends of HTML as need etc, and then you lightly use stimulus JS controllers for the small stuff where HTML/forms don't make sense. https://hotwired.dev/
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant comparison to established SSR-first approaches, reinforcing the discussion with a practical example.

comments.author: wg0
comments.text:
Have tried multiple approaches. Sevelte. HTMX. Everything in between.

And came to conclusion that after certain complexity of UI - React kind of components are unavoidable if you want to be able to reason about your code.

Think of Shopify admin panel with product variations each variation having different pictures and what not.

Not saying not doable in plain JS.

But then Chrome can be written in NASM too, C++ is just...
comments.analysis: Offers a pragmatic perspective on the necessity of component-based frameworks for managing complexity in large applications.

comments.author: cies
comments.text:
I went with SSR for a new project started 2025Q4. I'm not against React, but when I have to make an SPA (or very interactive component in an SSR app) I much rather use Elm.

In order to do the JS that I have to do with an SSR app I went with Alpine.js. It reduces the JS I have to write and that fits my SSR approach.

It feels a bit like a modern jQuery to be honest: you cannot build whole apps with it, but just to sprinkle on top of an SSR it does really well.
comments.analysis: Shares practical experience with SSR and lightweight JS frameworks, highlighting their strengths and limitations.

comments.author: egeozcan
comments.text:
IMHO, you shouldn't make "hate" part of your tagline.

Maybe focus on a use-case? Something like, "No-build, no-NPM, SSR-first JavaScript framework specializing in Time-to-interactive" - maybe?
comments.analysis: Constructively critiques marketing language, suggesting a more positive and use-case-focused framing.

comments.author: bearjaws
comments.text:
Why use JS at all for SSR?

It's not a great language for it.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about JavaScript's limitations for server-side rendering, though practical ecosystem factors often dictate its use.

comments.author: eknkc
comments.text: I thought I hated React until I saw the samples on this page...
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal change of opinion based on examples, reflecting subjective user experience rather than technical analysis.

comments.author: febusravenga
comments.text:
"If you hate react" feels like very bad argument in engineering.

Anyway, interesting approach for up to medium pages (not apps!). Totally not replacement for react.
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced critique of the marketing angle and realistic positioning of the framework's capabilities.
